name: Madrid Way of Saint James
name_original: Canal de Castilla via GR-89 desde Medina de Rioseco
description: Hike the moderate Madrid Way of Saint James in Canal de Castilla, covering 11.2 miles (18.0 km) with 132 feet (40 metres) elevation gain.
region_intro: Hiking around Medina De Rioseco offers a blend of historical charm and natural landscapes in the province of Valladolid. The region is characterized by the historic Canal de Castilla, providing flat towpaths, and the more varied terrain of the Montes Torozos. Riverside routes along the River Sequillo also contribute to the diverse outdoor experience. Expansive Castilian fields and traditional dovecotes define much of the surrounding Tierra de Campos. Best hiking trails around Medina De Rioseco The most popular hiking route is Madrid Way of Saint James, a 11.2 miles (18.0 km) trail that takes 4 hours 34 minutes to complete. This route follows a significant stretch of the historic Canal de Castilla. Another top favourite among local hikers is Castillos de Villalba de los Alcores y Montealegre de Campos — circular por la Tierra de Campos (GR 26), a moderate 10.9 miles (17.6 km) path. This circular route explores the wider Tierra de Campos region, known for its expansive fields. Local hikers also love the La Santa Espina from Castromonte — circular by the Senda del Embalse del Río Bajoz (SL-VA 74), a 11.5 miles (18.4 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 4 hours 45 minutes. Hiking around Medina De Rioseco is defined by the flat towpaths of the Canal de Castilla, the rolling hills of Montes Torozos, and riverside paths along the River Sequillo. The network offers options primarily for moderate ability levels. The routes in Medina De Rioseco are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from a few reviews. More than 30 hikers have used komoot to explore Medina De Rioseco's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hiking trails around Medina De Rioseco?
A: The hiking terrain around Medina De Rioseco is quite varied. You'll find predominantly flat and accessible towpaths along the historic &lt;strong&gt;Canal de Castilla&lt;/strong&gt;, which are ideal for tranquil walks. For those seeking more varied landscapes, the &lt;strong&gt;Montes Torozos&lt;/strong&gt; offer rolling hills and different vistas. Additionally, there are riverside paths along the &lt;strong&gt;River Sequillo&lt;/strong&gt;, often leading through natural spaces.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available in the area?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1085751304/castillos-de-villalba-de-los-alcores-y-montealegre-de-campos-circular-por-la-tierra-de-campos-gr-26" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillos de Villalba de los Alcores y Montealegre de Campos — circular por la Tierra de Campos (GR 26)&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 10.9-mile loop exploring the wider Tierra de Campos region. Another option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1085719914/la-santa-espina-from-castromonte-circular-by-the-senda-del-embalse-del-rio-bajoz-sl-va-74" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Santa Espina from Castromonte — circular by the Senda del Embalse del Río Bajoz (SL-VA 74)&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 11.5 miles through varied landscapes. You can also explore &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1195880956/palacios-de-campos-from-montealegre-circular-via-the-camino-del-tierra-de-campos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palacios de Campos from Montealegre — circular via the Camino del Tierra de Campos&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some notable natural features or points of interest to look out for on the trails?
A: The region is rich in natural and historical features. Along the Canal de Castilla, you can see engineering marvels like aqueducts (Fuente León, Fuente del Berro) and the historic San Antonio flour mill. The Montes Torozos offer viewpoints like Cerro del Moclín. The River Sequillo paths often lead to destinations such as the hermitage of Castilviejo. The wider Tierra de Campos region is also known for its traditional dovecotes and natural springs.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options around Medina De Rioseco?
A: While the guide primarily lists moderate routes, the flat towpaths along the &lt;strong&gt;Canal de Castilla&lt;/strong&gt; are generally suitable for families, offering easy walking conditions. The historical elements and tranquil scenery make for an engaging experience for all ages. The 'El Páramo - Medina de Rioseco' route, though not in the guide's listed routes, is described as an easy 3-mile walk with historical elements and pleasant groves, which could be suitable for families.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Medina De Rioseco?
A: The region experiences hot summers, so spring and autumn are generally the most pleasant seasons for hiking. In autumn, the expansive Castilian fields display remarkable ochre tones, offering particularly beautiful scenery. Spring brings milder temperatures and blooming flora. If hiking in summer, it's advisable to start early in the morning or late in the afternoon to avoid the midday heat.

Q: What kind of wildlife or flora might I encounter on the trails?
A: The region boasts a unique flora, especially in the Montes Torozos. The Canal de Castilla towpaths are shaded by numerous trees. The wider Tierra de Campos region is known for its rich native flora and birdlife. Along the 'El Páramo' route, you'll find clay slopes adorned with Aleppo pines and cypresses, and pleasant groves where birds sing.

Q: Are there any castles or historical landmarks near the hiking routes?
A: Yes, the area is rich in historical landmarks. You can find several castles nearby, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1789312" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montealegre de Campos Castle&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1789313" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Villalba de los Alcores&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5876325" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle Ruins of Tordehumos&lt;/a&gt;. The Canal de Castilla itself is an 18th-century engineering marvel, and routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1085008246/madrid-way-of-saint-james" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Madrid Way of Saint James&lt;/a&gt; pass through this historic waterway.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Medina De Rioseco?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Reviewers often praise the varied terrain, from the tranquil Canal de Castilla to the rolling hills of Montes Torozos, and the blend of natural beauty with historical charm. The well-maintained paths and opportunities to explore the unique Castilian landscape are frequently highlighted.

Q: How many hiking trails are available around Medina De Rioseco?
A: There are 8 hiking routes available around Medina De Rioseco, offering a range of experiences. All of these routes are rated as moderate in difficulty, providing engaging options for hikers.

Q: Is there public transport available to reach the trailheads?
A: While specific public transport details for individual trailheads are not provided, Medina de Rioseco is a town with some regional bus connections. For detailed information on reaching specific starting points, it's recommended to check local bus schedules or consider driving, as many trails in rural areas of Castile and León are more easily accessed by car.

Q: Are there any routes that follow the River Sequillo?
A: Yes, the River Sequillo flows through Medina de Rioseco and offers riverside routes for walking. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1195739237/castillo-de-tordehumos-from-villabragima-circular-by-the-rio-sequillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Tordehumos from Villabrágima — circular by the río Sequillo&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 6.7-mile circular route that allows you to experience the river's natural surroundings.

Q: Can I take a boat trip on the Canal de Castilla?
A: Yes, the Canal de Castilla is unique as the only navigable canal in Spain. Visitors can take boat trips on the 'Antonio de Ulloa' to appreciate the landscape from a different perspective, which can be a great complement to hiking along its banks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/890605" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal de Castilla Dock&lt;/a&gt; is a key point of interest for this.
tips: The Herrerian-style temple, from the end of the 16th century, was built according to the plans of Felipe de la Cajiga. The interior is made up of a wide central nave and chapels between the buttresses, whose baroque vaults were decorated by Felipe Berrojo.

The Traveler Reception Center, located in the Medina de Rioseco Canal dock, provides information regarding all the active tourism activities that run along this section of the canal: boat trips, electric boat rental, canoeing, hiking or routes cycling.
Today, it has become a suggestive tourist proposal to discover a surprising ecosystem, learn how the locks work and unravel the secrets of a flour factory dating from 1852.

Lock No. 7 of the Campos branch of the Canal de Castilla, located in Tamariz de Campos. Like all the canal's locks, its purpose was to allow barges to overcome uneven terrain, facilitating the transport of goods, especially grain and flour.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike the Madrid Way of Saint James?
faqs.answer: The most pleasant times to hike this trail are during spring and autumn. Summer months (July and August) can be very hot with limited shade, while winters may bring snow to the mountainous sections, particularly in the Sierra de Guadarrama.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Madrid Way of Saint James?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied. You'll encounter significant elevation changes in the initial stages, especially when crossing the Sierra de Guadarrama, which includes a challenging climb to the Puerto de Fuenfría. Beyond the mountains, the route becomes generally flat, consisting mostly of dirt roads, tracks, and footpaths, with some soft, sandy sections. You'll also walk on ancient Roman roads and Cañadas Reales (historic drove roads), with very little asphalt.

faqs.question: Is the trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The Madrid Way of Saint James is generally considered to be of medium difficulty. While much of the route is flat, the ascent over the Sierra de Guadarrama presents a significant challenge, especially stage four from Cercedilla to Segovia. Beginners should be prepared for some demanding sections and long distances between towns, requiring good physical condition and planning.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers diverse scenery, from urban environments to rolling hills, the dramatic peaks of the Sierra de Guadarrama, and extensive pine forests. You'll also traverse the vast Castilian Plateau. Along the way, you can visit beautiful historical towns and cities, such as Segovia with its Roman aqueduct, cathedral, and Alcázar, the Gothic Mudéjar style castle in Coca, and the Romanesque cloister in Santa María la Real de Nieva. Near Medina de Rioseco, you'll find the Church of Santa Cruz, the Canal de Castilla Dock, a Stone Bridge on the Canal de Castilla, and Lock No. 7 of the Canal de Castilla.

faqs.question: Is the Madrid Way of Saint James dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the official Camino de Santiago routes generally welcome dogs, it's essential to check specific accommodation policies along the Madrid Way of Saint James, as not all albergues or guesthouses may allow pets. Always keep your dog on a leash, especially near livestock or in populated areas, and ensure you carry enough water and food for them, particularly on longer, more remote stages.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a specific permit or to pay an entrance fee to hike the Madrid Way of Saint James. It is a public pilgrimage route. However, if you plan to stay in albergues (pilgrim hostels), you will need a 'credencial' (pilgrim passport) to access them, which can be obtained from pilgrim associations or churches before starting your journey.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and what are the parking options?
faqs.answer: The Madrid Way of Saint James officially starts in Madrid, typically from the Church of Santiago. For parking, you would need to find public parking facilities within Madrid, which can be challenging and costly. Many pilgrims opt to use public transport to reach the starting point. If you're starting from a later point, such as Medina de Rioseco, there are usually local parking options available in the town.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Madrid Way of Saint James is primarily part of the 'Madrid Way of Saint James' (97% coverage). It also significantly overlaps with the 'Canal de Castilla (Ramal de Medina)' (88%) and the 'GR-89 BTT Canal de Castilla (Ramal de Medina)' (88%). Upon reaching Sahagún, the route integrates with the more famous French Way (Camino Francés) for the final stretch to Santiago de Compostela.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the varied terrain and potential for long distances between services, essential items include sturdy hiking boots, layered clothing suitable for changing weather, a waterproof jacket, a hat, sunscreen, and a first-aid kit. Crucially, carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for stages with limited amenities. A pilgrim passport (credencial) is also recommended for accommodation. If hiking in summer, lightweight, breathable clothing is key, and in winter, prepare for cold and potential snow in the mountains.

faqs.question: Are there cafes, restaurants, or accommodation options along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail passes through numerous towns and villages that offer cafes, restaurants, and various accommodation options, including albergues (pilgrim hostels), guesthouses, and hotels. However, be aware that some stages, particularly in the more rural sections, can have long stretches between services. It's advisable to plan your daily stages and check for available amenities in advance, especially if you have specific dietary needs or prefer private accommodation.

faqs.question: What makes the Madrid Way of Saint James unique compared to other Camino routes?
faqs.answer: This route stands out for its origin in Spain's capital, offering convenient access. It is significantly less crowded than other popular Camino routes, providing a more peaceful and introspective pilgrimage experience. The trail boasts a rich history, following ancient paths used by various cultures, and its quieter nature often leads to more meaningful interactions with local communities along the way. It also features the highest elevation point of any Camino route in continental Spain at Puerto de Fuenfría.

waypoints.name: Stone Bridge on the Canal de Castilla
Lock No. 7 of the Canal de Castilla (Tamariz de Campos)
